title: Fitting a magnetic catch to a cabinet door
subtitle: Install a magnetic door catch inside a cabinet to hold a door shut without a latch or handle
excerpt: How to fit a magnetic cabinet catch. Covers positioning the magnet body on the cabinet interior and the steel plate on the door, adjusting the alignment for a flush-close, and the lateral adjustment on slotted fixing holes.
sourceNotes: UK joinery and cabinet-making trade practice. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A door catch that uses a strong neodymium or ferrite magnet housed in a plastic or metal body. The magnet body screws inside the cabinet on the shelf or partition; a matching steel strike plate screws to the inner face of the door. When the door closes, the strike plate is attracted to the magnet and holds the door shut without a mechanical click or resistance. Available in different strengths (light for small cabinet doors, heavy for wardrobe or utility-room doors. Push-to-open magnets incorporate a secondary release mechanism so a door with no handle can be opened by pressing and releasing.

content.text: holds a cabinet door closed without a visible latch or the alignment precision a ball catch requires. The approach is fixing the magnet body inside the cabinet first, then closing the door to mark the strike plate position from the magnet face.

Fit the magnet body to the underside of a shelf, the top of the cabinet base, or a fixed partition near the door) wherever it will align with a point on the door that has solid timber or ply behind it. Screw the magnet body in place with the screws provided, using pilot holes in hardwood or MDF.

Stick a piece of double-sided tape or a blob of Blu-Tack to the face of the magnet. Close the door firmly. Open the door (the strike plate or a marked impression shows where the strike plate needs to be on the door face. Position the strike plate over this mark and drive the screws. The strike plate slots are horizontal to allow lateral adjustment) do not tighten fully at first.

Close the door again and check that it holds shut with a light push and releases with a firm pull. If the catch is too strong or weak, many magnetic catches have a setscrew on the magnet housing to reduce or increase the magnetic force slightly. Once the holding force is correct, tighten the strike plate screws fully.
